bullets: Integrated six V7-style project demos into one Vibe ID format while preserving original screenshots, widgets, and artifact links.
Mapped each project to skill filters, deliverable evidence, demo surfaces, and methodology notes for faster navigation.
Reduced verbose project prose into a consistent reviewer order: evidence or deliverable, demo or screenshots, then method.
Maintained standalone HTML, local asset paths, and page metadata so each role version can run without a build step.

projects.navMeta: Creative brief automation | bilingual UI screenshots | worksheet artifact
projects.title: Creative Brief Automation: Structuring Brand-to-Design Campaign Handoffs
projects.tagline: AI-assisted workflow that turns loose marketing prompts into structured, copy-ready creative briefs for design handoff.

problemStatement.narrative: Brand and marketing teams need a faster way to turn vague campaign ideas into specific design direction before the handoff meeting.

approach.description: The workflow parses a natural-language prompt, extracts aesthetic intent, compares two style directions, and produces a structured brief.

result: Bilingual UI screenshots show the English and Chinese brief-generation flows.
Worksheet artifact documents the PRD, capability map, and feature priorities.
Final deliverable is a structured creative brief ready for designer handoff.

screenshots.alt: English-language version of the AI design strategy assistant
screenshots.caption: English UI · same workflow translated

screenshots.alt: Chinese-language version of the AI design strategy assistant
screenshots.caption: Chinese UI · bilingual workflow support

projects.summary: Turns a loose campaign prompt into a copy-ready creative brief with aesthetic intent, style options, role comparison, and bilingual UI screenshots.
owned: Scoped the one-screen prompt-to-brief flow for marketing and design handoff.
Defined the brief structure: aesthetic intent, style options, role comparison, and final direction.

stages.label: Evidence
stages.pmNote: Evidence comes first: the reviewer sees the actual brief flow and worksheet before reading method notes.

stages.label: Demo
stages.pmNote: The screenshots are the demo artifact for this project, so they sit before the methodology.

stages.label: Method
stages.pmNote: The method is simple and explainable: turn ambiguity into a constrained design brief.

projects.algorithmSummary: Prompt-to-brief pipeline: parse intent, extract visual language, compare styles, then generate a handoff-ready brief.

projects.navMeta: CNN microscopy | neural workbench | model confidence review
projects.title: Deep Learning for Microscopy Segmentation and Cell Classification
projects.summary: Microscopy CNN and segmentation workbench for reviewing masks, activations, confidence, and export decisions.
projects.algorithmSummary: Model-review logic: compare segmentation and classification outputs, inspect activations, and keep confidence visible before export.
owned: Built CNN-style review logic for microscopy classification and segmentation QC.
Connected predictions to visual inspection so collaborators can see failure modes.

endorsement.quote: The threshold sweeps changed the conversation from 'trust the model' to 'inspect the evidence and pick the operating range.'

stages.label: Evidence
stages.pmNote: The deliverable is the inspectable model decision, not a black-box prediction.

stages.label: Demo
stages.pmNote: The interactive workbench is the demo: it makes the CNN behavior visible.

stages.label: Method
stages.pmNote: The method emphasizes model behavior under noisy biological images.

widget.title: Inspect the deep-learning microscopy workbench
widget.help: Draw or load a microscopy-like signal, inspect layer activations, hide layers, and see how export confidence changes. This is the deep-learning project, not the ND2 agent.
widget.note: The workbench mirrors the deep-learning decision path: image signal, learned filters, pooled features, dense evidence, and a confidence-gated output.

problemStatement.narrative: Microscopy collaborators need model outputs that can be inspected visually, not only a final class label or mask.

approach.description: Compare segmentation models, inspect learned activations, and keep confidence thresholds visible before export.

result: Interactive neural workbench exposes layers, presets, confidence, and export state.
Source profile preserves the CNN classification result: 97% accuracy on 20,000+ samples.
Model comparison covers U-Net, Med-SAM, and DeepLabV3-style segmentation.

projects.navMeta: ND2 microscopy agent | FastAPI + React | threshold sweep demo
projects.title: ND2-Analysis-Pipeline and Imaging Analysis Agent
projects.summary: FastAPI + React agent for ND2 microscopy threshold review, cached previews, group statistics, and exports.
projects.algorithmSummary: Precompute threshold curves once, then let reviewers explore channels, ratios, statistics, and exports interactively.
owned: Built the agent-style FastAPI + React workflow for ND2 threshold analysis.
Standardized file scanning, channel mapping, cached previews, and group-level statistics.

endorsement.text: This is the correct agent project: it makes threshold selection and statistical reporting inspectable for scientists instead of hard-coding a single cutoff.

stages.label: Evidence
stages.pmNote: The evidence is the reproducible pipeline and exportable review package.

stages.label: Demo
stages.pmNote: The demo shows the key value: threshold choices update without rerunning the pipeline.

stages.label: Method
stages.pmNote: The method separates expensive preprocessing from fast reviewer interaction.

widget.title: Operate the ND2 threshold agent
widget.help: Switch channels and move the threshold. The preview, replicate distribution, statistical signal, and export status update together.

channels.label: Green channel
channels.note: Strong biological signal with visible group separation.

channels.label: Red channel
channels.note: Moderate signal; useful after checking replicate consistency.

channels.label: Green/Blue ratio
channels.note: Ratio view stabilizes intensity differences across images.

problemStatement.narrative: Scientists need to choose microscopy thresholds without rerunning a heavy image pipeline for every candidate cutoff.

approach.description: Precompute threshold curves and metadata once, then expose channel sliders, overlays, boxplots, statistical tests, and exports.

result: Public GitHub repo documents the ND2 analysis pipeline and setup path.
Interactive demo includes 0-4095 threshold control, channel views, ratios, and group summary.
Export path supports reviewable figures, settings, and statistical outputs.

projects.navMeta: SOM clustering | COPD phenotype topology | clinical interpretation
projects.title: COPD Phenotyping with SOM Clustering and R Shiny
projects.summary: SOM clustering workflow that groups COPD patients by lung-function patterns and makes phenotype differences inspectable.
owned: Applied SOM-style clustering to convert high-dimensional COPD features into interpretable groups.
Designed linked topology and cluster summaries for clinical review.

endorsement.text: This work stands out because the modeling, validation, and interface all stay aligned around clinical interpretability.

stages.label: Evidence
stages.pmNote: The deliverable is an interpretable phenotype map rather than a raw clustering score.

stages.label: Demo
stages.pmNote: The demo lets the reviewer inspect how each cluster changes under different clinical lenses.

stages.label: Method
stages.pmNote: The method uses topology to preserve similarity relationships across patient groups.

projects.algorithmSummary: SOM logic maps high-dimensional lung-function features into clusters, then validates patterns through linked clinical summaries.

widget.title: Explore how the SOM becomes a usable phenotype map
widget.help: Switch the analytical lens and click a cluster. The demo shows how topology, validation, and interpretability stay connected instead of being separate notebook outputs.

lenses.label: Age/BMI-aware
lenses.smartPoint: Age and BMI categories make the clusters clinically legible instead of numerically neat but hard to explain.
lenses.smartNote: This is the winning lens because it preserves the SOM geometry while giving clinicians a clearer reason why nearby patients belong together.
lenses.comparisonNote: Compared with the comorbidity-only setup, this lens gives cleaner phenotype stories without breaking topology quality.

comparison.label: Age/BMI-aware SOM
comparison.detail: Best balance of topology, separation, and clinical storytelling.

comparison.label: Comorbidity-only SOM
comparison.detail: Keeps some structure, but phenotype explanations are thinner.

comparison.label: Plain hierarchical clustering
comparison.detail: Produces groups, but loses the neighborhood map that makes review easier.

cluster-1.summary: Lower symptom burden and fewer comorbidities, useful as the low-intensity comparison phenotype.
cluster-1.why: This cluster gives clinicians a clearer low-burden anchor when interpreting the more severe groups.

cluster-2.summary: Metabolic-risk phenotype where BMI and age categories help separate patients who would look similar on comorbidities alone.
cluster-2.why: The added categories keep these patients from being collapsed into a generic mid-risk cluster.

cluster-3.summary: High-BODE respiratory phenotype with the clearest severity signal and strong separation from lower-burden groups.
cluster-3.why: This is the cluster clinicians can identify fastest because the severity signal is both statistically and visually obvious.

cluster-4.summary: Older multimorbidity phenotype whose neighborhood on the map shows it is closer to severe disease than to the low-burden groups.
cluster-4.useCase: Separates age-heavy multimorbidity from pure respiratory severity
cluster-4.why: The map neighborhood matters here: it explains why this cluster borders the severe phenotype without being identical to it.

cluster-5.summary: Younger obesity-linked phenotype that becomes much easier to explain once BMI categories are included in the feature space.
cluster-5.useCase: Shows why BMI-aware engineering changes the phenotype story
cluster-5.why: Without the BMI signal, these patients tend to disappear into broader mixed clusters.

lenses.label: Topology
lenses.smartPoint: Topology quality matters because a pretty cluster plot is not useful if nearby patients stop meaning similar patients.
lenses.smartNote: Frank keeps QE and topographic error visible so the chosen map is defensible on geometry before anyone starts telling a clinical story.
lenses.comparisonNote: This lens foregrounds that the SOM is doing more than clustering: it is preserving neighborhood structure well enough for the map itself to be interpretable.

comparison.label: 8x8 SOM grid
comparison.detail: Best compromise between quantization quality and stable neighborhood preservation.

comparison.label: 7x7 SOM grid
comparison.detail: Simpler, but compresses distinct phenotypes too aggressively.

comparison.label: 9x9 SOM grid
comparison.detail: Adds granularity, but the map gets harder to stabilize and explain.

cluster-1.summary: Low-burden region sits in a stable corner of the map with little leakage into high-severity neighborhoods.
cluster-1.useCase: Shows low-burden anchor stays geometrically clean
cluster-1.why: The cluster is useful because it remains localized instead of smearing across the map.

cluster-2.summary: Mid-risk metabolic region occupies its own band rather than being swallowed by neighboring groups.
cluster-2.useCase: Explains why the map can support a distinct metabolic subgroup
cluster-2.why: The geometry is stable enough that this subgroup remains identifiable even before the clinical layer is applied.

cluster-3.summary: Severe respiratory region forms the strongest connected neighborhood on the map, which is why it is the easiest cluster to trust.
cluster-3.useCase: Strongest proof that the SOM geometry supports the phenotype story
cluster-3.why: This cluster is central to the map and remains coherent across nearby units, which is exactly what HR should read as model stability.

cluster-4.summary: Older multimorbidity region is close to the severe island but retains its own neighborhood identity.
cluster-4.useCase: Shows map continuity rather than arbitrary hard walls
cluster-4.why: The neighborhood is informative because it explains adjacency without forcing these patients into the severe cluster.

cluster-5.summary: The BMI-sensitive lower-left neighborhood is more diffuse, which is why Frank needs the interpretability layer on top of the geometry layer.
cluster-5.useCase: Illustrates why topology alone is not the full story
cluster-5.why: This region is still valid, but it is the one that benefits most from adding the clinical interpretation layer.

lenses.label: Comorbidity only
lenses.smartPoint: This comparison shows why Frank changed the feature engineering instead of accepting the first clean-looking cluster plot.
lenses.smartNote: The comorbidity-only setup works, but the phenotype explanations are weaker because age and BMI effects get blurred into broader mixed groups.
lenses.comparisonNote: This baseline is useful because it proves the improvement did not come from prettier visuals alone; it came from a better feature space.

comparison.label: Comorbidity-only SOM
comparison.detail: Reasonable baseline, but phenotype narratives are broader and less specific.

comparison.label: Age/BMI-aware SOM
comparison.detail: Sharper, more clinically understandable phenotypes at similar topology quality.

comparison.label: Plain clustering
comparison.detail: Less map structure and weaker explanation of near-neighbor relationships.

cluster-1.summary: Low-burden group remains visible, but the neighboring mid-risk clusters become harder to explain without age/BMI context.
cluster-1.why: Useful, but less richly described than the final feature-engineered version.

cluster-2.summary: Mid-risk metabolic and respiratory profiles start to mix together when age/BMI structure is removed.
cluster-2.useCase: Shows what gets lost without the added categories
cluster-2.why: This is exactly the kind of cluster that motivated Frank's feature redesign.

cluster-3.summary: Severe respiratory profile still appears, but its border with adjacent groups is less clinically obvious.
cluster-3.useCase: Strong enough to see, weaker to explain
cluster-3.why: The severe cluster survives, but its phenotype story is flatter than in the final lens.

cluster-4.summary: Older multimorbidity patients and respiratory-heavy patients are less clearly separated here.
cluster-4.useCase: Good example of where the baseline underperforms
cluster-4.why: The neighborhood remains, but the phenotype explanation is much less distinct.

cluster-5.summary: The obesity-linked younger phenotype is the biggest loser in the baseline because the BMI signal is muted.
cluster-5.useCase: Best illustration of why Frank changed the feature space
cluster-5.why: Without BMI-aware features, this subgroup becomes much harder for clinicians to recognize.

problemStatement.narrative: Clinical reviewers need a way to inspect COPD patient groups across multiple lung-function and symptom features.

approach.description: Use SOM topology to cluster multidimensional clinical features, then inspect cohort lenses and cluster-level summaries.

result: Interactive SOM topology demo supports cohort lenses and cluster focus.
Five phenotype clusters summarize FEV1/FVC, exacerbation, symptom, and risk patterns.
Clinical review format makes clustering assumptions and interpretation visible.

projects.navMeta: Market Pulse Copilot | original screenshots + signal methodology
projects.title: Market Pulse Copilot for Market Monitoring and Ticker Briefs
projects.tagline: Market-monitoring prototype that organizes catalysts, price context, technical levels, and participation signals into a concise analyst-style ticker brief.

problemStatement.narrative: Market-facing users need a compact read of what changed, where price sits, and whether participation confirms the move.

approach.description: Compress three signal lenses into a scenario-aware brief for pre-market, intraday, and after-close review.

result: Four original screenshots show ticker input, catalyst context, signal framework, and final composite brief.
Screenshot sequence lets reviewers follow the product flow without the oversized demo console.
Source endorsement from a portfolio manager is preserved in the project card.

mentorReview.quote: Overall, this is a strong and impressive project. It already shows the structure of an agent-like system, and I believe this represents a meaningful future direction.
mentorReview.feedback: The mentor suggested expanding the model with additional factors, such as quality factors, to make the investment signal framework more robust.

screenshots.caption: Ticker Input + Market Scenario Selector

screenshots.alt: AAPL ticker brief with price context, company snapshot, and catalysts
screenshots.caption: Ticker Brief: Price Context + Catalysts

screenshots.alt: Market Pulse technicals, fund flow, and composite signal panels
screenshots.caption: Signal Framework: Technicals + Flow Context

screenshots.alt: Market Pulse final composite read and next watchpoints
screenshots.caption: Final Composite + Watchpoints

projects.summary: Market Pulse Copilot combines catalyst news, technical context, and fund-flow breadth into one sector-read demo.
owned: Structured the market workflow around catalyst, technical, and participation signals.
Packaged the Market Pulse story around original screenshots and a concise analyst-style brief.

stages.label: Evidence
stages.pmNote: Evidence comes from both screenshots and a preserved expert endorsement.

stages.label: Demo
stages.pmNote: The demo shows how the same ticker is read differently before open, intraday, and after close.

stages.label: Method
stages.pmNote: The method is useful because it prevents news-only interpretation.

projects.algorithmSummary: Scenario logic weights catalyst, technical, and flow signals differently before open, intraday, and after close.

endorsement.quote: Overall, this is a strong and impressive project. It already shows the structure of an agent-like system, and I believe this represents a meaningful future direction.

projects.navMeta: Lumina AI Product | screenshots | insight capture and theme library
projects.title: Lumina AI Growth Insight Companion
projects.source: AI Product Innovation Lab notes and provided screenshots
projects.tagline: AI-driven growth journal that turns fragmented thoughts into personal insight archives, themes, patterns, and reflection prompts.

problemStatement.narrative: Students and young professionals often lose useful self-reflections across notes, screenshots, conversations, and memory.

approach.description: Design a low-friction capture loop, classify themes and emotions, and surface recurring patterns through a personal insight archive.

result: Four original screenshots show capture flow, library, theme classification, and influence network.
MVP loop records thoughts, organizes themes, and generates reflection prompts.
Product notes cover user problem, cut features, blocker log, and role-capability mapping.

screenshots.alt: Lumina capture interface with tabs for instant recording, digital library, behavior patterns, growth insights, and growth biography
screenshots.caption: Capture Flow: Instant Thought Recording

screenshots.alt: Lumina digital life library showing core themes and structured insight archive
screenshots.caption: Insight Archive: Digital Life Library

screenshots.alt: Lumina theme classification view with relationship, career, expression, and value creation categories
screenshots.caption: AI Theme Classification

screenshots.alt: Lumina influence network view showing people and recurring themes tied to emotional patterns
screenshots.caption: Influence Network: People, Themes, and Emotions

projects.summary: AI growth insight product that captures thoughts, classifies themes, and surfaces recurring behavior patterns.
owned: Scoped the MVP around capture, theme classification, pattern review, and growth reflection.
Designed the information architecture for a personal insight archive and influence network.

stages.label: Evidence
stages.pmNote: The evidence is visual and product-specific: the reviewer can inspect the actual screens.

stages.label: Demo
stages.pmNote: The screenshots are the demo surface for the Lumina product story.

stages.label: Method
stages.pmNote: The method keeps the product focused on pattern recognition, not broad life advice.

projects.algorithmSummary: Classification loop: capture a thought, tag theme and emotion, archive it, then surface recurring people, topics, and patterns.
